23: apodekatoun to heduosmon kai to anethon kai to kyminon kai aphekate ta baryteta tou nomou — you tithe mint and dill and cumin and neglect the weightier matters of the law — baryteta (weighty things, the heavy end of a scale) is the metaphor: some laws have greater weight than others. The three weighty matters named are krisis (justice/judgment), eleos (steadfast covenant-love/mercy), and pistis (faithfulness); these are the OT prophetic priorities (Mic 6:8, Hos 6:6) that the scribal tradition had flattened by treating every regulation equally.
1: tote ho Iēsous elālēsen tois ochlois kai tois mathētais autou — then Jesus spoke to the crowds and to his disciples — Tote (then) marks a decisive shift from controversy dialogues to direct address. The dual audience (crowds kai disciples) is unusual; the woes that follow are public indictment, not private instruction.
2: epi tēs Mōseōs kathedrās ekathisan — they sit on Moses’ seat — Kathedra Mōsēos (Moses’ seat) is a physical chair in synagogues representing authoritative succession to the Mosaic teaching office. Ekathisan (aorist: have sat down, have taken their seat) acknowledges the institutional authority. The tension is between legitimate office and corrupt practice.
3: panta oun hosa ean eipōsin hymin poiēsate kai tēreite — all therefore whatever they say to you, do and keep — The command is qualified immediately: kata de ta erga autōn mē poieite (but do not do according to their deeds). The problem is not their teaching authority but the hypocrisy of teaching without practicing.
4: desmeuo usin de phortia barēa kai dysbastakta — they bind heavy and hard-to-bear burdens — Desmeūō (to bind, bundle up) + phortia (loads, burdens) + barēa kai dysbastakta (heavy and difficult to carry). Tō daktylō̧ de autōn ou thelousin kinēsai auta (but with their finger they are unwilling to move them) — the contrast between imposing and alleviating: they use one finger metaphorically to add load while refusing to lift a finger to help.
5: platūnousin tā phylaktēria — they widen the phylacteries — Phylaktēria (phylacteries) = the tefillin boxes worn on head and arm during prayer (from Deut 6:8; 11:18; Ex 13:9,16). Larger boxes signaled greater piety; platūnō (to make broad/wide) criticizes competitive public display. Megalunousin ta kraspeda (enlarge the tassels) = lengthen the tzitzit on the prayer shawl (Num 15:38-39).
6: philousin de tēn prōtōklisian en tois deipnois — they love the place of honor at feasts — Prōtōklisia (first-reclining-place, seat of honor) = the couch nearest the host at a formal banquet. Prōtokathedrias en tais synagōgais (chief seats in the synagogues) = the front benches facing the congregation. Both are public-honor markers.
7: tous aspasmous en tais agorais — greetings in the marketplaces — Public greetings with the title Rhabbi (my great one, my teacher — from Heb. rab = great) conferred honor before witnesses. Kaleōsthai hypo tōn anthrōpōn Rhabbi (to be called Rabbi by people) — the passive klēthenai (to be called, to be addressed) is the coveted public recognition.
8: hymeis de mē klēthēte Rhabbi — but you, do not be called Rabbi — The aorist passive prohibition mē klēthēte is strong. The grounds: heis gar estin hymōn ho didaskālos (for one is your teacher) and pantes de hymeis adelphoi este (and you are all brothers). The community is horizontally structured under one Teacher (didaskalos), not vertically ranked.
9: kai patera mē kalēsēte hymōn epi tēs gēs — and call no one your father on the earth — Patēr (father) as a religious title (equivalent to spiritual father/founder of a school); heis gar estin hymōn ho patēr ho ouranios (for one is your Father, the heavenly one). The prohibition targets honorific titling that displaces divine fatherhood, not biological naming.
10: mēde klēthēte kathēgētai — nor be called guides/leaders — Kathēgētēs (guide, instructor, leader — a rarer term than didaskalos; used for philosophical and religious teachers who lead others). Heis gar estiñ hymōn ho Kathēgētēs ho Christos (for one is your Guide, the Christ) — Christ as sole authoritative teacher.
11: ho de meizon hymōn estai hymōn diakonos — the greater among you shall be your servant — The comparative meizon (greater) picks up the status-vocabulary of the Woes and redirects it: greatness is servant-function (diakonos = table-servant). This repeats the community principle from 20:26-27.
12: hostis de hypsōsei heauton tapeinōthēsetai — whoever exalts himself will be humbled — The divine passives (tapeinōthēsetai = will be humbled; hypsōthēsetai = will be exalted) are eschatological reversals enacted by God. The pattern inverts all social-honor logic and is a structuring principle throughout the Sermon and beyond.
13: ouai de hymin grammateis kai Pharisaioi hypokritai — woe to you, scribes and Pharisees, hypocrites — Ouai is a lament-curse (not primarily imprecation but prophetic grief over coming judgment). Hoti kleiete tēn basileian tōn ouranōn emprosthen tōn anthrōpōn (because you shut the kingdom of heaven before people) — kleiō (to shut, lock); they neither enter nor permit others entry (tous eiserchotas).
14: [v.14 absent in earliest manuscripts — NA28 omits; a textual insertion parallel to Mark 12:40 / Luke 20:47]
15: periagete tēn thalassan kai tēn xēran poiēsai hena prosēlyton — you travel the sea and the dry land to make one proselyte — Prosēlytos (proselyte, convert — from prosēlythō = to come to) = a Gentile who fully converted to Judaism including circumcision and immersion. The zeal is acknowledged; the indictment is that the product (huion geennēs = son of Gehenna) ends worse than before: diploteron hymōn (twice as much as yourselves).
16: hodoi typhloi — blind guides — Hodos (guide, one who leads on the path) + typhloi (blind) = a self-defeating combination. The Woe focuses on casuistry about oath-binding: swearing by the naos (inner sanctuary) vs. the gold of the naos. Jesus’ critique targets selective oath-binding that creates loopholes in solemn commitments.
17: mōroi kai typhloi — fools and blind ones — Mōros (fool, dull, stupid — a rare strong reproach; in 5:22 its use against a brother was condemned) used here by Jesus himself against the scribes in their capacity as false teachers. The rhetorical question: tis gar meizon estin ho chrysos ē ho naos ho hagiasas ton chryson (which is greater, the gold or the sanctuary that sanctified the gold?) inverts the reasoning: the sanctuary confers sanctity on the gold, not vice versa.
18: hos d’ an omōsē en tō̄ thysiastēriō̧ ouden estin — whoever swears by the altar is nothing — The same casuistry applied to the altar: swearing by the altar is dismissed as non-binding while swearing by the gift on it is binding. Opheilei (is obligated, owes) = carries legal force. Jesus dismantles the loophole structure entirely.
19: typhloi — blind ones — The repetition of typhloi emphasizes the moral blindness underlying the casuistry. Ti gar meizon to dōron ē to thysiastērion to hagiazōn to dōron (for what is greater, the gift or the altar that sanctifies the gift?) — the same logic as v.17: the sanctifying agent is greater than what it sanctifies.
20: ho oun omōsas en tō̄ thysiastēriō̧ omnuei en autō̧ kai en pasin tois epanō autou — whoever swears by the altar swears by it and by all things on it — Jesus’ resolution collapses all the casuistic distinctions: swearing by any part of the temple complex implicates the whole and ultimately God himself. The sequence builds through vv.20-22 to the same conclusion: all oaths invoke God.
21: kai ho omōsas en tō̄ naō̧ omnuei en autō̧ kai en tō̄ katoikounti auton — and whoever swears by the sanctuary swears by it and by the one dwelling in it — Ho katoikōn (the one who dwells) = God, whose Shekinah presence fills the naos. Every oath by the temple is ultimately an oath by the divine Presence.
22: kai ho omōsas en tō̄ ouranō̧ omnuei en tō̄ thronō̧ tou theou — and whoever swears by heaven swears by the throne of God — The final step: swearing by heaven invokes the divine throne. Jesus’ argument (drawing on 5:33-37) shows all oath-distinctions collapse into direct appeal to God; therefore straightforward speech (nai/ou) is required.
24: hodoi typhloi hoi diyōlizein ton kōnōpa — blind guides who strain out the gnat — Diűlizō (to strain through, filter) — wine was filtered to remove insects that would make it ritually impure. Kōnōps (gnat, mosquito) = the smallest ritually impure insect; kamēlon de katapinon tes (but swallowing the camel) = the largest land animal. The gnat/camel contrast is extreme comic hyperbole; both are unclean (Lev 11), but the proportions reveal absurd priority inversion.
25: katharizete to exōthen tou potēriou kai tēs paropsidos — you cleanse the outside of the cup and dish — Exōthen (outside) vs. esōthen (inside) — the Woe targets ritual purity practice that leaves the interior (gemousin ex harpagēs kai akrasias = full of extortion and self-indulgence) untouched. Harpagē (plunder, extortion) and akrasia (lack of self-control, incontinence) describe the moral contents contrasted with the clean exterior.
26: Pharisaie typhle katharison prōton to entos tou potēriou — blind Pharisee, first cleanse the inside of the cup — The singular Pharisaie (Pharisee) shifts from plural address to singular, making the charge personal. Hina genētai kai to ektos autou katharon (so that the outside also becomes clean) — interior purification naturally produces exterior cleanness; the reverse is impossible.
27: paromoiazete taphois kekoniamenois — you are like whitewashed tombs — Kekoniamenos (perfect passive participle of koniáō = to whitewash with lime) — tombs were whitewashed before Passover (Nisan) so pilgrims would not inadvertently touch them and incur corpse-impurity. Exōthen men phainontai horaioi (outwardly appear beautiful/clean) — horaios (beautiful, seasonable, timely) contrasts with esōthen de gemousin osteon nekrōn (but inside full of dead men’s bones).
28: houtos kai hymeis exōthen men phainesthe tois anthrōpois dikaioi — so you also outwardly appear righteous to people — Phainō (to appear, seem) vs. genuine reality: esōthen de este mestoi hypokriseōs kai anomias (but inside you are full of hypocrisy and lawlessness). Hypokrisis (stage-playing, pretense) and anomia (lawlessness, without-law-ness) are the twin interior conditions behind the righteous facade.
29: oikodomei te ta mnēmeia tōn prophētōn — you build the tombs of the prophets — The final Woe targets the self-deceiving claim that the current generation would not have killed the prophets as their ancestors did (legete: ei ēmetha en tais hēmerais tōn paterōn hēmōn). The tomb-building is presented as a claim to discontinuity with the murderous past.
30: ouk an ēmetha koinōnoi autōn — we would not have been partners with them — Koinōnōs (partner, sharer, participant) in the blood of the prophets. The second-class conditional (ei ēmetha... ouk an ēmetha) expresses a counterfactual, but Jesus’ response (v.31-32) shows their claim is self-indicting.
31: hoste martyreite heautois hoti huioi este tōn phoneusāntōn tous prophētas — so you witness against yourselves that you are sons of those who murdered the prophets — Martyreite heautois (you testify against yourselves) — their own admission of ancestry is the self-incriminating testimony. Huioi (sons) here = those who continue the character/pattern, not merely biological descendants.
32: kai hymeis plērōsate to metron tōn paterōn hymōn — and you fill up the measure of your fathers — Plērōsate (aorist imperative or prophetic aorist: fill up completely) — metron (measure) = the fixed capacity that must be reached before judgment falls (cf. Gen 15:16; 1 Thess 2:16). Jesus speaks as if the murder of himself is the act that completes the measure.
33: opheis genēmata echidnōn — serpents, brood of vipers — The same epithet John the Baptist applied to Pharisees (3:7; 12:34). Pōs phygēte apo tēs kriseōs tēs geennēs (how will you flee from the judgment of Gehenna?) — kriseos (genitive of krisis) + geenna (Gehenna = Valley of Hinnom, the place of eschatological fire).
34: prophētas kai sophous kai grammateis — prophets and wise ones and scribes — Jesus sends these emissaries (apōstellō = I send; first-person: Jesus himself sends, in divine authority). Three fates: apokteneite (kill), staurosete (crucify), mastigōsete (flog), diōxete (persecute) — the full range of violence against Christian missionaries anticipated.
35: haimatos dikaiou ekchynnomenou epi tēs gēs — righteous blood shed upon the earth — From Habel tou dikaiou (Abel the righteous, Gen 4:8) to Zachariou huiou Barachious (Zechariah son of Barachiah) — the canonical span from Genesis to 2 Chronicles (the last book in the Hebrew canon). Ekchynnomenon (being poured out, present passive participle) keeps the spilled blood as ongoing cry before God.
36: hēxei tauta panta epi tēn genean tautēn — all these things will come upon this generation — Genea autē (this generation) throughout Matthew’s Passion block refers to the contemporaries of Jesus; the fulfillment in AD 70 is the historical horizon. Hēxei (future: will come) is a judicial visitation term.
37: Iērousalom Iērousalom hē apokteinousa tous prophētas — Jerusalem, Jerusalem, the one killing the prophets — The doubled vocative (Iērousalom Iērousalom) is a lament formula (cf. 2 Sam 18:33; LXX repeated vocative as grief). Pōsákis ēthelēsa episynagagein ta tekna sou (how often I wished to gather your children) — episynagō (gather together) + hōs ornis episynagei tā nossia heautēs (as a hen gathers her chicks under her wings) — a maternal image of divine protection-longing.
38: idou aphietai hymin ho oikos hymōn eremos — behold your house is left to you desolate — Ho oikos (the house) = the temple; eremos (desolate, abandoned) echoes the Shekinah’s departure in Ezek 10-11. The divine aphietai (is left, abandoned — present passive) implies God’s withdrawal from the temple.
39: ou mē me idēte ap’ arti heōs an eipēte — you will by no means see me from now until you say — Ou mē + aorist subjunctive = the strongest Greek negation. Eulogēmenos ho erchomenos en onomati kyriou (blessed is the coming one in the name of the Lord) = Ps 118:26, the same acclamation from the Triumphal Entry (21:9); the future return will be greeted with the same words, but then as recognition.
